docs.title: 🌌 The Akashic Records
docs.text:
"A living archive of every thought, insight, lesson, and experience I’ve ever gathered."

Welcome to The Akashic Records — my personal knowledge management system, engineered with intention, powered by Dendron, and structured using the PARA and CODE frameworks.

docs.title: 🧠 Purpose
docs.text:
To capture, organize, distill, and express information across all domains of my life — from data engineering to mystic philosophy, from career goals to daily insights.

This vault is my second brain — designed for clarity, velocity, and depth of thinking.

docs.title: 🔮 Structure
docs.text:
Based on the PARA method:

  • 📁 Projects — Active, goal-driven tasks with a clear outcome.
  • 🗂️ Areas — Ongoing responsibilities & focus zones.
  • 📚 Resources — Notes, references, and raw materials.

  • 🗃️ Archive — Dormant or completed content.

  • Additional spaces:

  • 🌀 Inbox/ – Fleeting ideas and quick captures

  • 🧬 Templates/ – Reusable structures for consistency

docs.title: ⚙️ Powered By
docs.text:
• Dendron: Hierarchical, VSCode-native PKM built for scale and developer productivity.
  • VSCode Extensions: Customizable tooling ecosystem to optimize workflows.
  • Dataview-like Queries: Native hierarchical note queries for data-driven insight retrieval.
  • Graph Views: Visualize knowledge relationships at scale.
  • Integrated CLI and API: Automate, customize, and extend your PKM environment.

docs.title: 🧩 Methodology
docs.text:
Built on the CODE process:

  1. ✍️ Capture – Everything flows into Inbox or Daily Notes.
  2. 🗂 Organize – Notes are filed into the PARA hierarchy.
  3. 💡 Distill – High-signal content is refined & summarized.
  4. 🗣 Express – Ideas become outputs: guides, apps, talks, etc.

docs.title: 🧭 Current Focus Areas
docs.text:
• [[Streamlit]]
  • [[LLM Workflows]]
  • [[PKM Strategy]]
  • [[Spiritual Systems Design]]
  • [[Career OKRs Q2-2025]]

docs.title: 🧙‍♂️ A Note on the Name
docs.text:
“The Akashic Records” is a mythical compendium of universal knowledge. In this digital incarnation, it represents my own evolving source of truth — curated, connected, and always expanding.

“Do not seek the truth — only cease to cherish opinions.” — Zen proverb

docs.title: Welcome to Dendron
docs.text: This is the root of your dendron vault. If you decide to publish your entire vault, this will be your landing page. You are free to customize any part of this page except the frontmatter on top.

This section contains useful links to related resources.

  • Getting Started Guide

docs.title: dendron
docs.text:
#tag.pkm.dendron.quicknotes

Alright, you're diving into Dendron—the VS Code-powered brain extension—and you want the express lane. Perfect. Dendron isn’t your average note app; it’s a hierarchical, markdown-first, dev-friendly PKM system that behaves more like a knowledge graph crossed with a file system. Most people get lost in features. You're going to dominate the 20% of Dendron that gives 80% of its value—fast.

docs.title: 🚀 Dendron in Record Time: The Ruthlessly Efficient Mastery Plan
🎯 Phase 0: What’s the Point of Dendron?
docs.text:
Think like a dev, not a diarist.

  • 🌲 Hierarchies = Namespaces (not folders)
  • ⚙️ CLI + Commands = Fast note creation, linking, publishing
  • 🧠 Second brain = One vault, all domains, organized by schema
  • 💥 Speed over structure: Don't organize before you write—Dendron organizes as you write.

docs.title: 🛠️ Phase 1: Setup That Doesn’t Suck (30 Minutes Max)
docs.text:
Install Dendron the Right Way:

  1. Install VS Code
  2. Install Dendron extension
  3. Open Command Palette → >Dendron: Initialize Workspace
  4. Choose "Create Single Vault Workspace"

Done. That’s it. Skip multi-vaults unless you love complexity.

docs.title: 🔥 Phase 2: The 5 Moves of Dendron Mastery (Core 20%)
✅ 1. Ctrl+L – Lookup Notes, Fast
docs.text:
• Type project.devops.pipeline, and boom—it creates or opens that note.
  • This is your note creation, search, and edit all in one.
  • Use dot notation like a namespace: tech.linux.commands.top → creates structured hierarchy instantly.

docs.title: ✅ 2. Use the Dendron CLI
docs.text:
npx dendron-cli doctor
npx dendron-cli publish export
npx dendron-cli seed add github:username/repo
  • Get health checks, export to static site, or pull in “seed notes” (like starter kits).

docs.title: ✅ 3. Master the Daily Journal Flow
docs.text:
Use > Dendron: Create Daily Note

  • Automatically creates daily.2025.05.17 (or whatever date)
  • Set a hotkey for journaling. (Yes, automate it.)
  • Use templates to auto-fill headers/tasks.

docs.title: ✅ 4. Use Note References & Links
docs.text:
• [[project.devops.pipeline]] – link to another note
  • ![[note.image.png]] – embed images/diagrams
  • [[^block-id]] – block reference = Dendron sorcery

Every link is an atomic relationship—this builds your brain graph.

docs.title: ✅ 5. Planting vs Linking (🌱 vs 🔗)
docs.text:
• Plant a note: project.ai.models.transformers
  • Link existing: just use [[ ]]
  • Don't pre-structure—plant as you go. Structure will emerge.

docs.title: 🧩 Phase 3: Templates + Task System (1 Day)
docs.text:
• Create a .vscode/templates folder.
  • Add project.md, daily.md, meeting.md, journal.md
  • Then set hotkeys or command palette access for template use.

Templates = pre-fab scaffolding for your thoughts.

# {{title}}

## Context
- 

## Key Points

## Links
- [[related.note]]

docs.title: 🧠 Phase 4: Hierarchies = Taxonomy That Works For You
docs.text:
Skip folders. Just use dot notation:

  • project.client.acme
  • project.client.acme.meeting.2025-05-17
  • project.client.acme.requirements
  • project.client.acme.deployments.pipeline

This becomes auto-tagging + self-organizing.

Use Dendron: View Hierarchy to see the tree.

docs.title: 🌍 Phase 5: Publish Your Notes as a Site (Optional but Sexy)
docs.text:
npx dendron-cli publish export
  • Outputs your vault to a static site.
  • Add a netlify.toml or push to GitHub Pages for full public PKM glory.
  • Bonus: Add custom.css and theme your brain.

docs.title: 🛡️ Bonus Pro Moves (When You're Ready to Show Off)
docs.text: Move What It Does dendron.yml config Full vault control (publishing, schemas) schema.json Define types of notes (like Templater on steroids) Seeding Notes Pull in other people's vaults as modules Graph View Visualizes linked notes like a neural net Ctrl+Shift+P macros Power command execution via command palette

docs.title: 📈 The 20% Recap
docs.text: Feature Result Lookup Notes 10x speed in navigating + creating Hierarchical Tags Context-rich note structure Templates Instant scaffolding Daily Notes Workflow logging + journaling Graph Linking Contextual thought networks CLI + Publishing Portability + public knowledge sharing

docs.title: Want it in Obsidian?
docs.text:
Irony noted—but yes, I can port this cheat sheet to Obsidian.

Want a starter vault, custom template kit, or automation scripts (CLI or hotkeys)? Say the word and I’ll drop it like it’s hot.

docs.title: Venv
docs.text:
Creating a virtual environment in Python is like giving your project its own private sandbox—no package chaos, no version drama, just clean, isolated bliss. Here's how you do it, step-by-step:

#tag.python.environment.venv.quicknotes

docs.title: 🐍 Step-by-Step: Create a Virtual Environment in Python
✅ 1. Check Your Python Version
docs.text:
Make sure Python is installed:

python --version
# or
python3 --version

You need Python 3.3+ for venv.

docs.title: 🛠️ 2. Create the Virtual Environment
docs.text:
In your project folder:

python -m venv venv

This creates a folder named venv (you can name it anything) that contains a copy of Python + pip.

💡 Naming convention tip: Use .venv or env to keep it hidden-ish or project-specific like venv-mkdocs.

docs.title: Windows (cmd.exe):
docs.text: venv\Scripts\activate

docs.title: Windows (PowerShell):
docs.text: .\venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1

docs.title: macOS/Linux:
docs.text:
source venv/bin/activate

You’ll know it’s activated when you see (venv) in your terminal prompt.

docs.title: 📦 4. Install Packages
docs.text:
Now you’re working in isolation. Go ahead:

pip install mkdocs mkdocs-material

docs.title: 💾 5. Freeze Your Dependencies
docs.text:
For reproducibility:

pip freeze > requirements.txt

Others can replicate your environment with:

pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: ❌ 6. Deactivate When Done
docs.text:
Just:

deactivate

Back to global Python—no mess.

docs.title: 🔄 Quick One-Liner (for automation nerds)
docs.text:
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate && pip install -r requirements.txt

Perfect for bootstrapping a project.
